title: Generative Models in the Real World
concept: Deploying generative models requires balancing latency (feed-forward generator), output safety (filters), and monitoring for distribution shifts.

exercises.prompt: What is the most important part of deploying generative AI models to real users?

options.text: Making sure the AI can draw cute cartoon animals.
Balancing speed, safety filters, and monitoring for changes.
Finding the biggest computer possible to run the AI.

exercises.explain: Speed (latency), safety (guardrails), and adapting to new data (monitoring) are key to running AI in the real world.

cards.title: AI in the Wild!
cards.body: Building a cool AI model on your computer is just the first step. But what happens when millions of people start using it at the same time? Let's explore how we make generative AI fast, safe, and reliable for the real world!

cards.title: Real-World AI Deployment
cards.body: Deploying AI in the real world means making sure the model responds quickly (low latency), stays safe by filtering out harmful text/images, and is monitored to ensure its performance doesn't drop over time.

cards.title: The Fast-Food Drive-Thru
cards.body: Think of deploying an AI like opening a fast-food drive-thru. The generator is the kitchen speed (latency) getting burgers to hungry drivers. The safety filter is the quality inspector at the window stopping bad orders. Monitoring is the store manager watching for changes in customer tastes over time!

cards.title: The AI Email Writer
cards.body: An AI email assistant uses a lightweight generator to write drafts in milliseconds. Before the user sees the draft, a safety filter checks it for offensive language. Behind the scenes, engineers track how often users rewrite the drafts to see if the AI needs retraining.

cards.title: Myth: Set It and Forget It
cards.body: Many people think once an AI model is built and tested, it is done forever. In reality, real-world data is constantly changing! We must constantly monitor models for 'distribution shift' to keep them from getting outdated and inaccurate.

cards.title: Unmanaged vs. Production-Ready
cards.body: Deploying AI requires balancing speed, safety, and continuous updates. Here is the difference between an unmanaged model and a production-ready model.

left.text: Slow responses, no safety checks, and breaks when trends change.

right.text: Fast latency, safety filters active, and monitored for shifts.

exercises.prompt: In real-world applications, what does 'latency' refer to?

options.text: The accuracy of the AI's translation
The safety rating of the generated images
The time delay before the AI generates a response
The amount of computer memory the AI takes up

exercises.explain: Latency is the delay between a user asking a question and the AI starting to answer. Lower latency means a faster, better experience!

exercises.prompt: True or False: Once a generative model is deployed, you never need to monitor it or update it again.
exercises.explain: We must constantly monitor deployed models because user behavior and real-world data change over time.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the term for when real-world data changes over time.
exercises.sentence: When users start talking about new topics that the AI wasn't trained on, we call this a distribution [blank].
exercises.explain: Distribution shift occurs when real-world user data changes, causing our trained AI model to perform poorly if it isn't updated.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following scenarios describes an AI safety filter in action?

options.text: An AI that writes recipes using only raw, unfiltered honey.
A system that checks a generated image for harmful content and blocks it if found.
An algorithm that speeds up image loading times on a website.

exercises.explain: Safety filters analyze inputs and outputs to block inappropriate content before it ever reaches the user, keeping the AI application safe.

exercises.prompt: Your team is launching a new AI chatbot to millions of users. What are the three most critical things you must manage to ensure a safe, fast, and reliable user experience?

options.text: Quick response times, safety filters for bad content, and tracking changes in real-world data.
Only making the model larger and using more training computers.
Ignoring real-world user data and hoping the model never makes mistakes.
Blocking all user inputs entirely to prevent any possible errors.

exercises.explain: To launch a generative model successfully, you need low latency (quick responses), safety filters to block harmful outputs, and continuous monitoring to catch when the real world changes (distribution shift).
